body_txt: This trope refers to the habit of movies based on TV shows casting the main characters as being involved in a dramatic plot, particularly when nothing remotely similar happens in the series itself. This can be as big as the world or as small as recess, just so long as it is made "epic". Oddly enough, often seems to involve neighborhoods being torn down to build shopping malls. Due to the fact that nearly all movie adaptations use this trope to some degree (after all, it's easier to keep up an epic tone across a 90-minute movie as opposed to a 13-52 episode season), examples shall be limited to things that involve a large change in dynamic. Although it is common to conflate the Big Damn Movie with The Film of the Series , some do note distinctions between the two. The Film of the Series is more of a modernized remake of the TV show's concept with a completely new cast, the original living TV actors being too aged or out of relevance to reprise their roles. note For many shows with kid characters, by the time the big budget movie based on their show comes around, they are around the same age as their former adult co-stars who, themselves, are elderly or deceased at that point. While it is, technically, a big damn movie in the sense that being contemporary, it will make use of modern filmmaking and visual techniques that are prohibitive or nonexistent on a television budget (especially in terms of a television show that is decades old by that time), it is still a brand new, updated, rebooted continuity and a reimagining of the characters and premise; for example, The Addams Family (1991) . The Big Damn Movie , in comparison, features the original cast and if being made at some point after the run of the TV show, it still serves as a form of continuation, even taking into consideration the aging of the actors and acknowledging the ones who are no longer with us . Can sometimes overlap with Summer Blockbuster , but is distinct from an Epic Movie , which is inherently epic to begin with. Sequel Escalation is a similar phenomenon. May be advertised with an In-Theater Poster .
